sentences.text: In a small area west of Washington Square, the streets seem chaotic and split into narrow spaces called "places."
These locations create unusual angles and curves that can surprise and intrigue those who observe them.
One street crosses itself once or twice, creating a confusing path for those who walk through it.
An artist once found a valuable opportunity in this street, although many people failed to see its potential at that time.
Imagine a painter with a bill for materials suddenly meets himself coming back, and he has not paid anything at all!
Soon, the creative people arrived in the charming old Greenwich Village. They searched for north-facing windows, eighteenth-century gables, Dutch attics, and affordable rents.
They brought in some pewter mugs and a few chafing dishes from Sixth Avenue. Therefore, they formed what they called a "colony."
At the top of a short, three-story brick building, Sue and Johnsy lived in their studio.
Johnsy was a name that Joanna knew well and felt a strong connection to, therefore it held special meaning for her.
One person was from Maine, while the other person came from California, which is known for its beaches and sunny weather.
They met at a restaurant called Delmonico’s on Eighth Street, where they enjoyed similar tastes in art and food. Therefore, they decided to share a studio together.
That event happened in May, which is often a time of blooming flowers and warmer weather, making it a special month for many people.
In November, a cold and invisible stranger, known as Pneumonia, moved through the colony, touching some people with icy fingers.
On the east side, this strong figure walked with confidence, attacking many victims as he moved slowly through the narrow, moss-covered paths.
Mr. Johnson often felt lonely in the busy city, although he enjoyed walking in the park, where he could see friendly faces.
Although pneumonia is often viewed as a serious illness, it is not something noble or honorable like a chivalric gentleman.
A small woman, whose strength faded in the warm California winds, was not a fair match for the old man with rough hands and short breath.
However, Johnsy lay still on her painted iron bed, gazing through the small Dutch window at the blank wall of the next brick house.
One busy morning, the doctor invited Sue to come into the hallway, where his shaggy, gray eyebrow caught her attention.
"She has a one in ten chance of success," he said, while he shook the mercury in his clinical thermometer.
"That opportunity is for her to truly desire to keep living, even when life is hard and challenging."
The way people stand in line by the undertaker makes everything in the pharmacy seem foolish and unimportant.
Your little girl has decided that she will not get better, although she has a chance to recover.
Is there something troubling her thoughts at the moment?
"She always dreamed of painting the beautiful Bay of Naples one day," said Sue.
“Would you like to use paint for your project, or do you prefer another material?"
"His reaction was one of disbelief and frustration, as he couldn't understand why things turned out this way."
Does she have something important on her mind that she should think about more, like a man, for example?
"Is that a man?"
"said Sue, with a twang in her voice that sounded like a jew's harp."
“Is a man truly valuable? However, the doctor disagrees; he believes there is no such thing.”
"Well, it seems that this is the weakness, then," the doctor said with a serious expression.
"I will do everything I can to achieve what science is able to do through my efforts."
However, whenever my patient starts to count the carriages in her funeral procession, I reduce the number by half.
Although medicines can help people feel better, their healing power is sometimes misunderstood by those who seek quick fixes.
If you can convince her to ask one question about the new winter cloak styles, I will give her a better chance. Instead of one in ten, she will have one in five.
After the doctor left, Sue went into the workroom and cried into a Japanese napkin until it was completely torn apart.
Then she confidently walked into Johnsy's room with her drawing board, happily whistling a lively ragtime tune.
Johnsy lay quietly under the blankets, her face turned towards the window, looking outside at the world.
Sue stopped whistling, although she thought that she was already asleep and lost in her dreams.
She set up her drawing board and started a pen and ink picture for a magazine story.
Young artists must create their own paths in art by illustrating stories written by young authors trying to succeed in literature.
While Sue was drawing an Idaho cowboy in stylish riding trousers and a monocle, she suddenly heard a strange, low sound repeating several times.
She hurried to the bedside, although she felt a mix of worry and hope about what might happen next.
Johnsy's eyes were wide open, showing her surprise or fear, however, she remained still in that moment.
She looked out the window, and although she seemed calm, she was counting backwards to focus her thoughts.
“Twelve,” she said first, and then a little later, she counted down to “eleven,” followed by “ten,” “nine,” “eight,” and finally “seven.”
Sue looked out the window with concern, hoping to see something that would ease her troubled mind.
"What was left to measure or consider in a world where everything feels uncertain and fleeting?"
The yard looked dull and empty, while the plain wall of the brick house stood just twenty feet away.
An old ivy vine climbed halfway up the brick wall, although its roots were gnarled and decayed over time.
The cold wind of autumn had blown the leaves off the vine. Therefore, the nearly bare branches clung to the crumbling bricks.
"What is it, my dear friend?"
"Although Sue was curious, she hesitated to ask her friend for help because she feared the answer might not satisfy her."
"Johnsy whispered the number 'six' softly, reflecting her serious mood and the heavy feeling in the room."
"They are dropping more quickly now, which makes everyone feel worried and anxious about what might happen next."
Three days ago, there were nearly one hundred people gathered at the event, creating a lively atmosphere that everyone enjoyed.
Counting them caused a big headache for me, although I tried to focus and continue.
However, it has become simple now.
Another one has just passed by, although it feels like time is moving quickly and we barely notice these moments.
Now, there are only five remaining, although many people once hoped for more options in the past.
"Although you are curious, I wonder what you mean by 'five' in this context, my dear."
Please share your thoughts with Sudie, as she would appreciate your perspective on the situation.
Although leaves fall from trees, they symbolize change and the passage of time in nature's cycle.
The ivy vine climbs up the wall beautifully, although it often needs sunlight and careful watering to thrive well.
When the final one falls, I will have to leave as well.
I have been aware of this fact for the past three days, although it has not changed my feelings.
Did the doctor not inform you about that?
“Oh, I have never heard such nonsense,” Sue complained, showing her strong dislike and surprise at the idea.
"What do the old ivy leaves have to do with your recovery, although they hold memories of the past?"
Although you were a naughty girl, you used to really love that vine very much.
"Don't act foolishly, as it can lead to trouble and misunderstandings in many situations."
This morning, the doctor told me that your chances of getting better soon were quite high. He mentioned they were ten to one!
That is almost as good a chance as we have in New York when riding streetcars or walking by new buildings.

Please drink some broth now. Meanwhile, let Sudie return to her drawing. She hopes to sell it to the editor and buy port wine for her sick child. She also wants to get pork chops for herself, as she often feels greedy.
"You do not need to get more wine," Johnsy said, as she kept her eyes focused on the view outside the window.
"There goes another one of them, drifting away quietly, leaving behind only memories that make us feel nostalgic and somewhat alone."
No, I do not wish to have any broth at this time, although I appreciate your offer.
That means there are only four remaining items left for us to consider, however, they are still important choices.
I want to watch the last one fall before darkness comes, therefore I feel a sense of urgency.
Then, I will also join you.
"Johnsy, my dear," Sue said while leaning over her, "will you promise to keep your eyes closed and not look out the window until I finish working?"
I need to submit those drawings by tomorrow, however, I feel a bit overwhelmed because I have many other tasks to complete.
I need the light to stay bright; however, I would pull down the shade if it were too bright.
"Could you draw in the other room, although I understand it is comfortable here?"
"Johnsy asked in a cold voice, showing no warmth or kindness to those around her."
"I would prefer to stay here with you," Sue said, showing how much she valued their time together.
"However, I don't want you to continue staring at those foolish ivy leaves."
"Please tell me when you have finished," said Johnsy, closing her eyes and lying still like a white statue, "because I want to see the last leaf fall."
I feel very tired from waiting for a long time, and I no longer have patience.
I feel exhausted from thinking too much about everything that is happening around me.
I want to let go of everything that holds me back, and sail down gently like a tired leaf drifting in the wind.
"Although Sue spoke softly, her words carried a sense of urgency as she encouraged him to find rest."
"I need to call Behrman to be my model for the old hermit who worked as a miner."
I will not be away for long.
"Please remain where you are until I return, although I will be back soon to help you."
Old Behrman was a painter who lived on the ground floor, just below their apartment.
He was over sixty years old and had a long beard like the one on Michelangelo's Moses, draping down his body like a satyr's.
Although Behrman dreamed of being a great artist, he often felt like a failure in his art career.
For forty years, he used the brush, but he never got close enough to touch his Mistress’s robe.
Although he always planned to paint a great masterpiece, he had never started it yet.
For many years, he created nothing but occasional paintings for business purposes or advertisements.
He made some money by being a model for young artists in the colony who could not afford to pay a professional.
He drank too much gin, however he still spoke about his soon-to-be great work of art.
He was a fierce old man who strongly disliked weakness in others. He saw himself as a special guard for the two young artists above.
Sue found Behrman in his dim, poorly lit room, where he had a strong smell of juniper berries around him.
In one corner stood a blank canvas on an easel, waiting for twenty-five years to welcome the first line of art.
She shared Johnsy’s dream with him and expressed her fear. She felt light and fragile, like a leaf, worried that she might drift away as her grip on life weakened.
Old Behrman, his red eyes full of tears, loudly shouted his scorn and mockery for such foolish ideas.
"Vass!" she shouted, feeling both excitement and worry at that moment, as her heart raced with anticipation and fear.
"He shouted in distress, feeling overwhelmed by his emotions and unable to control his tears."
"Is there really anyone in the world who is foolish enough to die just because leaves fall from a vine?"
I have never heard of anything like that before, although it sounds quite interesting and unusual to me.
No, I will not act as a model for your foolish and simple-minded hermit.
"Why do you let such silly thoughts disturb her mind and create confusion in her life?"
Oh, that poor little Miss Johnsy is truly in a difficult situation. Although she tries to stay strong, her sadness is very noticeable.
"She is very sick and weak," Sue said. "The fever has made her thoughts strange and filled with odd ideas."
Very well, Mr., I understand your concern; however, I believe we can find a solution together.
Behrman, although I would like you to pose for me, you do not have to if you don't want to.
However, I believe you are a very annoying and foolish old person.
"You are similar to a woman, which can mean strength and passion, although it might not always be accepted by everyone."
"Behrman shouted loudly, expressing his frustration and anger about the situation he was facing."
"Who claimed that I will not be strong or assertive?"
"Please continue speaking, as I am eager to hear more about what you want to share."
I will go with you.
For thirty minutes, I have been trying to say that I am ready to begin.
God!
This is not a place where someone as good as Miss Yohnsy should be sick.
One day, I will create a great piece of art, and we will all leave together.
"God!"
Yes, I agree with you about this matter. Therefore, it is important to consider different perspectives.
Johnsy was resting soundly in her bed while they quietly made their way upstairs to check on her.
Sue lowered the shade to the window-sill, and then she signaled for Behrman to come into the other room.
They looked out the window with fear, watching the ivy vine that climbed the wall outside.
They shared a silent glance, acknowledging each other's presence, although words were not needed at that moment.
A constant, chilly rain was falling, and it mixed with snow, creating a cold and wet atmosphere outside.
Behrman, wearing his old blue shirt, sat down on an upturned kettle, imagining himself as a hermit-miner searching for a rock.
When Sue woke up after sleeping for an hour the next morning, she saw Johnsy staring blankly at the closed green shade.
“Pull it up; I want to see,” she whispered, giving her order with a sense of urgency and curiosity.
Although Sue was tired, she chose to obey and follow the instructions given to her.
However, suddenly!
After the heavy rain and strong winds that lasted all night, one ivy leaf still stood out against the brick wall.
It was the final fruit left on the vine, which reminded me of the end of summer.
Although it was still dark green near the stem, its edges showed hints of yellow decay. It hung bravely from a branch about twenty feet above the ground.
“It is the final one,” Johnsy said, expressing a sense of finality in her voice.
"I believed it would certainly drop during the night, although the darkness made everything seem uncertain and unclear."
I heard the wind as it whispered through the trees, bringing both calm and a feeling of mystery.
It will fall today, and I will also die at the same moment.
"Dear, dear!" she exclaimed, feeling a mix of worry and surprise that made her heart race.
“Think of me,” Sue said softly, leaning her worn face close to the pillow, “if you cannot think of yourself.”
What should I do in this situation?
However, Johnsy remained silent and chose not to respond to what was being asked of her.
The most lonely thing in the world is a soul preparing for its mysterious and distant journey.
The desire seemed to take control of her more and more as her connections to friends and the world faded away.
The day slowly passed, and even as twilight came, they could still see the lone ivy leaf hanging on its stem by the wall.
As night arrived, the strong north wind blew again, while rain continued to hit the windows and drip from the low Dutch roof.
When it was bright enough, Johnsy, who was very tough, ordered that the shade be lifted.
The ivy leaf remained quietly in its place, although many changes happened around it in the garden.
Johnsy lay in bed for a long time, gazing at the wall as her thoughts drifted away.
She called out to Sue, who was busy stirring her chicken broth on the gas stove.
"I have not behaved well, Sudie," Johnsy admitted, feeling remorseful and aware of the impact of her actions.
"Something has kept that last leaf there to remind me how cruel I have been."
Although some may feel hopeless, wanting to die is often seen as a serious mistake and brings deep sadness.
You can bring me some broth now, along with milk mixed with a bit of port. However, first, bring me a hand mirror. Then, please pack some pillows around me. I will sit up and watch you cook.
An hour later, she finally spoke, expressing her thoughts clearly and honestly, although her feelings were still intense and mixed.
“Sudie, I dream of painting the beautiful Bay of Naples one day, although it seems far away.”
The doctor arrived in the afternoon, and Sue found a reason to walk into the hallway as he was finishing.
"Although the doctor held Sue’s thin, shaking hand, he tried to reassure her that there were equal chances for recovery."
"Although good nursing is important, you will succeed if you take care of yourself and follow the right advice."
Now, I need to check another case that is waiting for me downstairs.
Behrman is his name, and I think he is some kind of artist, although his true talent is not well known.
Pneumonia is a serious illness that can affect many people, often leading to hospitalization and health struggles.
He is an elderly and frail man, and the situation he faces is very serious.
Although there is no hope for him, he goes to the hospital today to feel more comfortable.
The following day, the doctor told Sue that her friend was no longer in danger, which brought her relief.
You have achieved victory, which brings joy and excitement, although it may also lead to new challenges ahead.
Nutrition and care are important now, although many people often forget their value in daily life.
That afternoon, Sue went to the bed where Johnsy lay, happily knitting a blue scarf that was not very useful, and hugged her gently.
"I have something important to share with you, little white mouse,” she said quietly, her voice filled with emotion."
“Mr."
Behrman passed away today in the hospital because of pneumonia, which was a serious illness that affected his lungs.
He felt unwell for just two days, but during that time, everything seemed difficult and overwhelming.
The janitor discovered him on the first morning of his stay. He was in his room downstairs, feeling helpless and in great pain.
His shoes and clothes were completely soaked, and they felt very cold against his skin.
They could not understand where he had gone on such a terrible night, which seemed to scare them all deeply.

They found a lantern that was still lit and a ladder dragged from its place. There were scattered brushes and a palette with mixed green and yellow colors. Meanwhile, dear, look out the window at the last ivy leaf on the wall.
Did you ever think about why it never moved or fluttered when the wind blew?
Oh, my love, this is Behrman's greatest work of art. He created it on the night when the last leaf fell from the tree.
